teamObjective.explanation: Opening lead with only low non-trump spades. The priority is to shed the weakest card at the lowest cost, preserving the 4-6 for future tricks while opponents spend higher cards.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 cards, equal to both opponents, so no strong signal about strength. With no public plays, partner could be strong or weak; a low lead gives them the cheapest chance to beat it and take control.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each hold 3 cards and have shown nothing. Given my hand is 3-6 of spades with no trumps, it is likely at least one opponent can beat any card I lead. They may try to win cheaply or force us to spend our slightly higher cards.

actionRationale.primaryReason: The 3 of spades is the weakest card, so leading it sheds the least valuable card and keeps the 4-6 for later tricks. A low lead also costs opponents the most if they choose to win, and gives partner the widest range of cards that can beat it.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 expends the strongest card in a weak hand. If an opponent or partner takes the trick, I am left with only 3-5 and even less chance to win future tricks.
The 5 is still a middle card and does not shed as cheaply as the 3. It preserves less strength than the 3 lead while being just as beatable.
The 4 is only slightly better than the 6 or 5, but the 3 is strictly lower and therefore the superior shed card with no downside.

risks: Any opponent likely beats the 3 of spades with a higher non-trump or any trump, so we will probably lose the lead.
If partner cannot beat the trick, we may face a strong return lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Because all four cards are low non-trumps, losing this trick is acceptable. By playing the 3 we minimize the information revealed and keep our best cards for later in the round.

notes: Standard Guandan opening principle with a weak hand: lead the lowest card to shed cheaply.
